purpose: Evaluates the availability and effectiveness of four historical deleveraging channels under current structural conditions to determine which paths remain open for DM sovereign debt resolution.
components: Financial repression / negative real rates: partially available but constrained by capital mobility, digital alternatives, and agricultural supply shocks. Operating band narrowed from ~4-5pp to ~2-3pp.
Strong real GDP growth: impaired by demographics but potentially reopened by AI productivity. Structural vs cyclical determination of current ~2.7% labor productivity is the key uncertainty.
Primary surplus: blocked by entitlement spending at 14-15% of GDP vs 3-4% in 1945. IMF base rate for required consolidation <10%.

Inflation erosion: constrained by above-target starting point, agricultural supply shocks, and narrowed operating band. Above-target inflation sustained for years required for meaningful deleveraging.
current_assessment: Only financial repression remains clearly available, at reduced effectiveness (1-2pp/year vs 4pp/year post-WWII). Overall structural similarity to the post-WWII template estimated at 0.35. The modal outcome is a slow, multi-decade deleveraging via repression with periodic interruptions.
limitations: Post-WWII is N=1 for successful advanced-economy deleveraging at this scale
Modern capital mobility has no historical parallel for comparison
AI productivity could reopen the growth channel in ways not captured by historical analysis
Channel interactions are poorly understood — channels may substitute or conflict
Biofuel mandates may structurally narrow the repression operating band in ways without historical parallel
